EURRENT AFFAIRS.

The Irish Language Movement has entered into the
Trish National system, and, as a result, though the
Gaelic League, as a distinct organisation, may unfor-
tunately show signs of weakness, it does not follow at
all that the Irish Language Movement is not going
ahead and broadening its base as the years roll on.
Last Saturday and Sunday witnessed a remarkable
series of Feiseanna at Kells, in the County of Meath,
at Kilkenny, at Cahirciveen, at Castlebellinghiam, at
Wicklow, and at Nenagh. The Bishop of Killaloe, the
Most Rey. Dr. Fogarty, speaking at the Nenagh Feis
on Sunday, remarked:—‘‘ Well, it may be asked if
the Irish language is so valuable and dear to the Irish

pecple, why did they ever give it up? The answer is
that the language of every nation will be of necessity
the language taught in the primary schools, and the
decath sentence of our National language was pro-
nounced the day the National tongue was banned in
the National Schools. It is a remarkable fact that the
50 years of National decadence in prosperity. and in
population have been precisely the 50 years in which
the Trish language has ceased to be the language of the
pecple. Whether we have here a case of mere coin-
cidence or of a cause and effect it may be for others
to say, but the fact is there, and it is a significant one.
The one effective way to give back to this country its
own language is to have it universally taught in all
primary schools.”’

His lordship summed up the whole case for the lan-
guage revival very concisely in the following sentences:—
‘“ With the revival of the Irish language will come back
the emancipation of Irish genius, the expansion and
development of Irish ideas, and the strengthening of
the Trish character. Ireland was never destined by
nature to be a mere imitator of other people’s thoughts.
If you go to any other nation of the world you will
see the National life reflected in all the tracings of
National life—the houses they build, the clothes they
wear, the language they speak, the music they admire,
their arts, industries, customs, and pastimes, all bear
upon them the peculiar stamp of National character,
taste, and thought. A nation which is capable only
of imitation, or which is doomed to mere imitation,
is doomed. to destruction, for imitation means feeble-
ness, stagnation, and vulgarity.  For the last fifty
years the life of Ireland for the most part has been one
of most undignified imitation. It took its ideas,
its standard of taste, and beauty in almost everything,
as it took its clothes—ready-made from England.
There was not in Europe a people so capable of origina-
lity as the Irish people, or who have a mind so rich
and fertile in ideas. The object of the Gaelic League
movement, of which this great Feis is' destined to be a
most important episode, is to emancipate Irish thought
from this position of abject inferiority and inactivity ; to
stimulate Irish ideas and to develop healthy and charac-
teristic National life, and in this, the surest of ways,
to lay firm and square the foundation of a prosperous,
united, and self-respecting Irish Ireland. May God
speed the cause.”

Amongst those who spoke at the Nenagh Feis was
Mr. T. P. Gill, of the Department. This is as it
should be, for the more the Department is in touch
with Irish life the better for it and the better for the
country. The Feis at Kilkenny was opened by Capt.
Otway Cuffe, the Mayor of that ancient city, to whose
efforts the existence of the Kilkenny Woollen Mills is
largely due. Captain Cuffe was assisted at the opening
by the Countess of Desart, Baroness Prochazka, the
Hon. Mrs. Cuffe, and the members of the" Corporation
iu their robes. At this rate of going the ‘elite’” of
Rathmines may shortly come to the conclusion that the
Irish language is a thing they may safely know! The
Cahirciveen Feis was opened by the Very Rev. Canon
O’Riordan, P.P., V.F. Dr. Windle, President- of

YQE:(U,,C.,_ who has thrown himself with great heart into
Irish life, performed the opening ceremony at the
- Castlebellingham Feis. Unfortunately, at this season,
man proposes, but the rain postpones, and so the
Castlebellingham Feis was put off by torrents shortly
after it was opened. Sir Henry Bellingham is Presi-

* dent of the Feis Committee, and we note that amongst

those present was Lord Castletown. Tiord Castletown,
as our readers ought to remember, is Grand Secretary
of the Secret Society known as the Free and Accepted
Masons of Ireland, so that his presence at the Feis is
emphatic evidence of the breadth of the Gaclic League
programme. Personally we have a very poor estimate
of Brother Castletown as a public man, but on the
Gaelic League platform ““all are welcome.”

On Saturday the French Pavilion at the ‘‘ Interna-
tional > Exhibition was opened. We have not seen it,
but we do not suppose that any of the effects of the
anti-religious campaign of that country are on show.
A cabin from which tenants had been evicted is an
appropriate picture of Ireland of recent years, and no
doubt the contemporary history of France under
Clemenceau and Co. could provide many pictures as a
result of the French Government’s crow-bar brigade on
the religious houses.

There was a dinner in the evening at which such
nobility as Sir J. W. Goulding, Mr. Justice Ross, Sir
Jehn Nutting, of the “ Scholarships;’’ Sir James Ien-
derson, “Professor Mahaffy, G. Dent, Percy B. Ber-
nard, and W. H. Brayden, Editor of the ‘ Freeman,”’
were present. Mr. William Murphy, who runs the
“ Nationalist organ,”” made a speech. According to
the report in his paper, ‘ The Independent,’” he said—
“It was one of the compensations for his labours in
connection with the Exhibition that to these labours
was due the honour of being called on to address such
a distinguished assemblage.”” Surely a waiter called
‘upon to make a speech when he had cleared the table
could not have been more obsequious. We give Mr:
Murphy every credit as an able business man in the
tram line of business, but we would feel more respect
for him if he stood by his native Bantry rather than
grovelling at ““.the honour of being called on to address
such a distinguished assemblage.”

- Mr. William Murphy, who runs ‘“The Independent,’’
a self-styled ‘‘ Nationalist organ,’’ also declared in his
speech :—‘‘ His Excellency, in proposing the health of
the President of the Republic, truly said that the
friendship of the Irish and the French peoples, was of
long duration, and there was not an Irish peasant,
however unlettered, and however ignorant of written
history, who had not a strong traditional sympathy with
the French Nation.”” Ts it not dishonouring the bones
of the noble dead to drag the ‘“ Wild Geese’’ from
their graves,in order to prop up this flunkey enter-

tainment?  What of modern I'rance, Mr. Murphy,
of the “ Nationalist organ?”’
The Lord Lieutenant proposed ¢ The President

of the French Republic.”” The Lord Lieutenant,
who, of course, is only a puppet in these matters,
declared :—‘ They had all heard of the ‘entente cor-
diale.” He ventured to say that in Ireland it was a
matter of history and of long duration. ~Now, their
English friends were following theu‘lex_ample, and re-
cognising its beneficence.”’  Raimeis is no word for

The Session 1907-8 at the' Agricultural College,
Mount. Bellew, Co. Galway, commences on September
3rd next. An Entrance Examination must be passed
in order to gain admittance to the CfJllege. The De-
partment award twenty £10 scholarships, and students

holding scholarships need only pay £15 for the session;

other students pay £25. The fee for non-boarding
students is £3. About four hours daily—Sundays and
Saturdays excluded—are spent at practical work on the
farm ; two-and-a-half hours weekly are devoted to hor-
ticulture ; and bee-keeping and a little wood work are
also taught. Indoors there is the laboratory work ;
there is a course of chemistry and physics, valuation of
manures and feeding stuffs, farm seeds, veterinary
hygiene, botany. The Mount Bellew Agricultural Col-
lege is under the management of the Franciscan
Brothers.

Messrs Alexander Finlay, Ltd, soap and candle manu-
facturers of Belfast, write us:—“ We take the liberty of
sending you samples of our ‘ Emerald ’ wax candles, and
an imitation ‘ Imerald’ wax made in England, and
beg to draw your attention to the inferior quality of
the imitation, also its bad appearance and burning
qualities. We would, also, point out that maker’s
name is withheld on label used, and as this is fre-
quently done by English houses to deceive the Irish
customers, they should insist on seeing the maker’s
name. Such traffic as this merely brings discredit on
Irish goods, for customers naturally suppose that they
are of Irish manufacture. ~ We have written to the
makers and sellers of these goods and have received
apologies and promises to discontinue the infringe-
ment of our registered name ¢Emerald’ for wax
candles.”

We have received No 2, Vol I. of ‘“The Belvederian.”
As a mechanical production it is, indeed, excellent.
There is an article entitled ‘“ A Belvedere Artist,”” illus-
trated by a picture by the artist, and a picture of the
artist. We thought there was something familiar about:
it; it was like a dream to us that we had heard the
name Mr. V. de Gernon before. Our recollections be-
came definite when we vead that this * Belvedere
Artist ”’ had a picture on exhibition at ‘the Gaelic
Gallery,” 12 Westmoreland Street, Dublin. ““ The
Belvederian,”” which, no doubt, is scarcely less an
authority on art than it is on deportment, refers to Mr.
de Gernon’s ‘‘famous picture.”” It says: “It was
very highly spoken of by the Dublin papers.”’  ““The
Belvederian ” thereupon quotes, ‘of all papers in the
world, “The Freeman’’ on this ‘“famous picture.’”
In March, 1901, upwards of six years ago, Mr. de
Gernon advertised in the LEAaDER the exhibition of
what the ‘‘ Belvederian’’ calls his ‘ famous picture.”’
We said nothing about this ‘ famous picture,” and so
Mr. de Gernon wrote us a letter in which he com-
plained that ‘T sent the advertisement under the im-
pression that I would receive, at least, as much notice
as was accorded by the rest of the Dublin press.”  We
published Mr. de Gernon’s letter and commented on it B
we protested against being classed with *“ the rest of the
Dublin press;” we pointed out that we did not give
notices in exchange for advertisements 5 we remarked

The Rev. Mr. Hannay, of Westport, has turned funny
man. In a British paper, the “Pall Mall Gazette,”
he contributes an article which he calls a ° County Mayo
Comedy.”’”  This member of the Executive of the
Gaelic League also recently contributed an
aiticle to a special Humphrey’s Dump number of a
British monthly. We understand his sympathies are
with Mr. Martyn’s Party! ‘“A County Mayo
Comedy,” written with a view to making Cockneys
laugh at ““ Oireland,” is all about some row, or alleged
row, between an Irish Member of Parliament and an
Archdeacon of the ‘‘Idolatrous’’ Church. The Rev.
libeller of Foxford Convent had a congenial dish to cook,
and spice for the palates of the British readers of an
anti-Trish paper in London—an Irish M.P. and a
‘“ Romish priest.”” And if the Rev. Mr. Hannay, mem-
ber of the Executive of the Gaelic League, got a guinea
for cooking and spicing the dish, no doubt he will con-
sider he earned the British money fairly ; for what more
Irish nation-building work could any pure-souled patriot
—nay, even of the hero type who magnanimously ‘steps
out of his class’’ and joins the Gaelic League—do
than endeavour to raise a few ignorant Cockney
laughs at the expense of goms, priests, and other such
characters from the County of Mayo. There is a
reference in the article to.‘“ a Dr. Connor Maguire.”
we believe, a member of the
Executive of the Gaelic League, and has done a consider-
able amount of work for Irish literature. But, then,
what is such a person compared with an Episcopalian
Rector, when' the latter is endeavouring to make Cock-
neys laugh at the expense of Mayo? It was conde-
scension on the Rev. Mr. Hannay’s part to refer to him

as ““a Dr. Connor Maguire,”” rather than say as ‘“‘a

- priest-ridden lout,”” or some such choice expression. At

this particular juncture in the relations between Eng-
land and Ireland, it is highly patriotic work for a mem-
ber of the Executive of the Gaelic League to endea-

_vour, in,the columns of a British paper, to make
‘Cockneys laugh at the alleged buffoonery of Mayo

priests and people.  Referring to the alleged dulness

says—‘‘The Suffragettes? They are poor, tame creatures
besides our priests and guardians.”’” If the Rev. Rector
of Westport, in the County of Mayo, reads “.A County’

An exhibition of Irish industries under the auspices
of the local Irish Industrial Association was opened at
Waterford on Wednesday of last week. Captain Cuffe,
performed the opening ceremony. We are glad to note
that the exhibits were numerous and representative of
many Irish industries. In a circular issued by the Com-
mittee in connection with the exhibition the following
appeal is divected to the women of Ireland:—‘‘ Please
remember that with you lies the buying power, and
censequently in your hands also lies the success of the
Irish industrial movement. Come to the exhibition.
See for yourselves what can be done at home, and in
your future purchases, when at all practicable, insist
on getting goods of Irish manufacture.”” We are sorry
to say it—but we would rather be truthful than
gallant—that Irish women need a lot of appealing to
in this respect. One of the great advantages of the
Trish-Treland movement is the important avenue of
national work it has opened for Irish women. ‘The
buying power,”’ referred to in the circular of the
Waterford Industrial Association, in the hands of Irish
women  could now be made a very effective weapon in
the battle for Ireland.

The paper and discussion on the Catholic Defence
Society at ~the Maynooth Union are opportune.
We are denied Self-Government largely because ‘‘ Eng-
land’s Faithful Garrison in Ireland ’’ have the ear of
and the pull on the ‘‘predominant partner,”’ and
whilst ready to cheer for the Boers and that sort of
thing, we plainly have not pluck enough to stand up to
“ England’s Faithful Garrison in Ireland’’ on the soil
of Ireland. It is not merely want of nerve that keeps
us from the fight; there are fallacies that impose on
obtuse and immature minds placed in the way also; it
is said to be ‘sectarian’’ if the ¢ Idolators’’ stir in
their chains, it is likely to embitter feeling—that is, in
plain language, embitter the ‘‘ascendancy.”” ‘‘Extreme’’
men will fire Wolfe Tone and Emmet and Parnell at
your head and tell you that these men were better Irish
patriots than Peter the Packer, and so forth, as if such
palpable and admitted facts had any thing to do witl
the contention that if the Catholics of Ireland want
justice, tolerance and fair play they must unite and
fight for them. Monsignor Hallinan had ne difficulty
in showing that the Catholics are boycotted largely in
their own land. He quoted from the articles in the
LEeaper which were, subsequently, issued under the title
““ Three Railways and a Bank.”” Monsignor Hallinan,
after a recital concerning the position of the  ascen-
dancy,” remarked :—‘‘ Little wonder that the ascen-
dancy’ party should cling to the Union with grim
tenacity.”” Little wonder, indeed; but it is a great
wonder that the Catholics, in face of this situation, do
not stand up to their real and immediate enemies, that
self-same ‘“ ascendancy.”

of the most up-to-date in the world. The Irish Yeast
Ccmpany, 6 College St., Dublin, and 54 Waring St.,
Relfast, are sole agents for Ireland. We notice they
ave giving away copies of instructions for using the
yeast in home baking, and-any person who applies to
6 College St., Dublin, for free sample will be supplied.

out that amongst the perquisites offered to the much-
sought-after Protestant Royal Engineer pensioner . for

This year the Pilgrimage to Croagh Patrick will take
place on Sunday, July 28th. -An interesting document
has been found in the Papal Archives in Rome con-
cerning this historic pilgrimage.  Father MacDonald,
the Adm. of Westport, sends us a copy of the text of
it in English. It runs as follows:—‘‘ 27th day of Sep-
tember, 1432. Pope Eugene IV. grants to the Arch-
bishop of Tuam an indulgence of two years and two
quarantines on the usual conditions, for those penitents
who visit and give alms towards the repair of the fabric
of the Chapel of St. Patrick on the mountain which is
called Croagh Patrick; this indulgence to be gained
on the Sunday preceding the Feast of St. Peter’s
Chains ; because on that day a great multitude resorts
thither to venerate St. Patrick in the said Chapel.”
Here we have documentary evidence of this famous pil-
grimage dating back nearly five hundred years.

THE DEPARTMENT IN BEING.

III.—THE TECHNICAL CONGRESS.

HE Technical Congress has now completed its fifth
session. What is the Technical Congress?

Well, as its name implies, it is in the first place a
Congress concerned with Technical Education in Ire-
land. It has a Standing Committee, just as other im-
portant bodies have, and that Standing Committee meets
the authorities at the Department at various times—
we take it when the Committee has anything special
about which to confer with the Department.  The
Technical Congress has no direct powers, and must not
be confounded with the Board of Technical Instruc-
tion. It is a voluntary body composed of delegates
from the various Committees, and its funotion is to dis-
cuss and advise and get into immediate touch with the
Department through its Standing Committee. The
Department this year, in the persons of Mr. T. P. Gill
and Mr. Fletcher, came into touch with it, and both
contributed to the proceedings, particularly Mr.
Tletcher, who, as secretary in respect of Technical In-
struction, is naturally more intimately concerned with
the deliberations of the Congress. It stands in a
somewhat similar relation to the Department as does
the Committee of the Heads of Secondary Schools. As
evidence that the Department welcomes all the con-
sidered and informed advice that it can get, it may be
set down that the expenses of the delegates are paid
out of the Joint Fund, that is, the fund made up of
the Department’s grant and the local contributions.
We have heard that the Congress last week was the
best yet held; we cannot offer a first-hand opinion on
the matber ourselves; as it was the first we had the
pleasure of attending. The value of such a Congressis,

We were not able to be present at the first day’s

hours at the meeting on Wednesday of last week, and
we did not feel the time going.

During the relaxation of electing a Standing Com-
_mittee, the Congress broke up for the time being. They

were good humoured, indeed, jolly-looking delegates.

. The president, Dr. Windle, has, as least, the face of a
humorist, the Hon. Secretary, Father Dowling, C.M.,
looks like a man quite accustomed to taking opposition
and criticism as part of the day’s work. There were
several priests, one or more Protestant clergymen, many
Northerners, and a number of bronzed men from the
rural parts. There was also a pretty baby girl, of about
six or seven years, sitting on a lady-delegate’s lap. She
was too pretty to suggest a budding suffragette, but,
anyway, her technical education was being commenced

" at a very early age.

The election of some members of some Committee or
other was carried on by a special group. A delegate
near us had up his hand. ‘I don’t know who I am
voting for,”” said he. ‘“ You don’t take your duty
seriously then,’’ we remarked. ‘Sure, when you don’t
know any of them, what else can you do?’ was the
retort. Ile was the flippant exception that emphasised
the earnestness of the others.

The discussions kept up to a high average level, and
the speeches were remarkably free from flamboyancy or
green flaggery. The delegates were serious men, who,
in their respective localities, were adopting the policy
of making the best of such Home Rule as we have in
respect of technical instruction. There are only two
ways of getting a comprehensive view of the Annual
Technical Congress—one is to attend it, and the other
to read the official report of its three days’ deliberations
when it is published. Unfortunately, there are only a

~ comparative few so interested in the efforts to promote

_ technical education in Ireland prepared to do the one
or the other.

On the second day of the Congress the question of the
most effective type of scholarship suited to Ireland was
discussed at great length, and with much display of plain
practical ability ; the note throughout was thoroughly
practical. It was emphasised that one of the main
objects of technical scholarships was to improve the
industrial condition of the country. Mr. Forth, from
Belfast, laid it down as a general principle that, apart
from the position of the boys, or rather of the boys’
parents, the boys who won the scholarships needed them
most, and he gave some interesting statistics that tended
to bear out this view. Ior instance, in Belfast it was
found that the average rateable value of the houses in
which boys who won certain £10 scholarships resided
was about £11, whilst of those who won prizes value £2
the rateable valuation was about £17. This, he con-
tended, went to show that, as.a matter of fact, it is the
peor boys who are likely to work hard and win the
technical scholarships. For there is a view that these

confined to those

-whose parents are not well off. The figures quoted by
Mr. Forth were in support of the view against any

‘arbitrary restriction or ‘‘ protection’’ of scholarships

and in favour of allowing full ‘“free trade’ in them.

.D'u‘:"l'ng this discussion Father Breen, of Clare, empha-

sised what he considered the weakness, from a practical
point of view, of the existing system of scholarships as
they worked out in rural districts; they did not neces-

in so far as they failed in doing that, they failed in
accomplishing the purpose for which they were created.
In rural districts a boy from the bogs, having won a
scholarship, got a year’s technical teaching, and as a
result was landed in a quagmire. There was no qecul:;ty
that boys who got scholarships turned to industrial life,
and he ‘insisted that the Department should allow the
Committee to grant apprentice scholarships. The sym-
pathy of the Congress appeared to be quite with that
view, and it was, subsequently, embodied in a resolution
and passed.

Father Dowling, C.M., is a most unusual type of
‘“able and energetic Secretary,”’ inasmuch as he is
both energetic and able. He gave ‘‘education’’ as
she, or it,. manifests herself, or itself (no one could re-
gard education in Ireland as a masculine thing yet), a
well-deserved drubbing. It is too literary,”” and
turns the nation’s heads towards clerkships rather than
towards workshops. By the way, onme result of this
misdirection of education that has accompanied the
cffort to make an Industrial Revival is that, while we
do not hear of many new industrial firms starting des-
pite all our talk, there have been quite a plethora of
“No. 1, vol. 1’s’’ established which fizzle out after
a brief and inglorious existence. Our ‘‘ literary ”’ edu-
cation leads many inexperienced youngsters, who have
been diverted from industrial careers, to think that
any fool can run a journal of opinion. Father Dowling
talked slashing common-sense concerning the trend of
Primary and Intermediate and University Education
in Ireland. And that fact leads us to the reflection
that it is really not common sense we lack in this
country—the lack is more apparent than real—but we
lack the power to shape things according to our com-
mon sense. There are very few people even amongst
the producers of this ‘‘literary’’ education in the
Primary and Intermediate Schools who would not say
ditto to Father Dowling, and yet they have perforce to
go on day after day grinding boys to be clerks and
things of that sort in a country which is literally starv-
ing for mechanical skill and knowledge with a utility
value. The Intermediate Colleges are all struggling for
cxistence and for result fees, and the Castle Boards call
the educational tune and the Colleges dance accordingly.
It is not a sense of what the country needs that we lack,
but the power to run education along the lines of that
sense. Iather Dowling admitted that the influence of
the Department was tending to turn the tide somewhat,
and if for no other reason than that it is desirable, in
our opinion, for Ireland to take all it ¢an out of the
Department and make the best of it. Iather Dowling
is quite hot on this miseducation of the country. All
sensible men would be hot about it if their attention
were directed to it as is the attention of Father Dowling,
who has specialised in the subject. The country is cool
because, having so little power in these matters, it has
grown callous. The prizes, exhibitions, and so forth,
Father Dowling pointed out, head education in the
wrong direction; even the College of Science errs in
this_direction in his opinion.

However, it is not our purpose in this article to deal
with the views expressed at the Congress, we merely
wish fo show the class of institution the Technical Con-
gress is. The discussions were marked with frankness
and independe‘nce.. We detected little or no spirit of
Lostility to the Department, though it got some
adyice for ifs own good, which, as all of us know, is
seldom, or ever, véry flattering. T

THE CATHOLIC TRUTH SOCIETY.

A T the late meeting of the Catholic Truth Society

the President, Most Rev. Dr. Healy, adverted
strongly, and not for the first or even second time, to
the desirability of publishing booklets dealing with Irish
history. As the matter has now been a couple of years
under consideration since it was first mooted at an
annual meeting, it would be interesting to know what
practical progress, if any at all, has been made in
materialising the expressed wishes of the Archbishop
and of other members of the Council, backed up by the
Catholic and National Press in general, on this sub-
ject of Irish history. True, there were prizes offered,
following the last annual conference, for booklets on
certain events in our annals, etc.; but there has been
no intimation since of any further progress having been
made. Exception was taken at the time in the LEADER
to two of the subjects chosen—‘‘The Red Earl of
Ulster *’ and ‘‘ The Invasion of Edward Bruce,”” both
in the same period of our history, and with, from the
Catholic point of view, something actually malapropos
in their selection. 5

Why not, as suggested then, give us Brian Boru,
Hugh O’Neill, Owen Roe, Rory O’Moore, or Patrick
Sarsfield, to begin with? Any one of these, with a
picture of the subject on the cover, would go off like
wildfire. It is wonderful how well even Mrs. William
O’Brien’s ‘‘ Under Croaghpatrick,” with ¢ Murrisk
‘Atbey ’’ on the green cover, sells. But 1 once heard
that there were some wsthetes on the Committee ‘of
Management who deemed these cover-pictures vulgar,
although the English C.T.S. utilises them largely, and
for Irish biographies, too, such as ‘“ St. Patrick *’ and
‘“ Father Tom Burke, O.P.”” And are we to take it
that the lives of Irish patriots, and the history of im-
portant and stirring events in our annals, are more
harmful than the wild and improbable—often immoral
—tales which fill so much of the place that might be
occupied by them in the reading pabulum of our youth
—those stories of detectives and thieves and pirates and
bandits and burglars, imported by the ton into this
country ?

If the element in the Committee of Management,
with the historical prejudices and proclivities of the
Board of National (?) Education, interpose any further
obstacles to the publication of booklets, treating of
the really important periods and patriots of our past,
the work may be taken in hand, as a mere business
speculation, by British Protestants, who ar.e'ready any
day to manufacture even arms and ammunition for the
very enemies of their own country so long as there is
money to be made by it. b

IRISH SERMONS IN THE EIGHTEENTH
CENTURY.*

HE course of studies at Maynooth College is so
full and so exacting and the time allotted to it
so limited that there is necessarily but little leisure
for what might be called hobbies or by-studies. One
cennot appreciate too highly, therefore, the zeal and
industry that prompted a small band of these students .
to undertake and put into execution the work of editing
a series of Irish sermons in two volumes from the
manuscripts that are preserved in the College Library.
The work of editing Irish matter from manuscripts is
slow and tedious, and often beset with practical diffi-
culties of a serious character. It may be said at once
that considering the circumstances under which the two
volumes before me were produced, and considering the
““Opge’’ of the editors, the work of editing them has
been performed with reasonable accuracy and judg-
ment, the second volume being more carefully done
that the first. The editors promise a third volume,

hiich: will complete the series. May I suggest that in

“all the three, pointing out the extent to which the
manuscripts have been departed from in the matter of
sorthography, ete. Also, as far as possible, some
account of the authors of the sermons, and the sources
~ from which they drew their materials. Of the sermons
in these two volumes some were written in the first
half of the eighteenth century, others later in the
same century.  Of others again the dates cannot be
fixed with certainty, though many of them were, of
~ course, composed long before the copies used by the
-editors were made.

It is difficult to read over these sermons without a
sense of sadness and wounded national pride. Through-
out the eighteenth century Irish, as a cultivated lan-
guage, was on the wane; writers became careless in the
use of words and idioms; the very penmanship deterio-
rated. A feeling of despair seemed to have taken
Pessession of scribes and authors. There was practically
1o printed Irish to help to form a general standard.
Thl_a only literary exercises that received attention were
lyrics and elegies in poetry and Fenian stories in prose.
The prose cssay, as it was understood in English or
TFrench, had no existence in Irish. Men with literary
instincts broke out into verse, and evinced no desire
to excel in prose. Prose was not appreciated, it was
scarcely recognised at all as literary form. Writers did
not care to put their names to prose compositions, as
‘they did not believe they individualised them as poetry
-did. Why should not one man’s prose be like another’s?
‘What did it matter who wrote prose? The information
given was all that mattered. Nevertheless, there was
prese of real value in the stories that passed from mouth
to mouth. But the people were almost unconscious of
its excellence. What I have said of prose in general
is true of the species of prose known as oratory, sacred
and secular. Of secular oratory we have scarcely any
specimens dating from the eighteenth century, unless
we consider mock speeches like that put by O’Rahilly
into the moath of Tadhg Dubh as such. Of sacred
-oratory we have some specimens left, but they scarcely
-do justice to the genuine native eloquence that flourished
during the period in question. No doubt the writers
of sermons like those in the volumes before me never
-expected them to be printed and published, and prob-
ably they were intended rather as foundants on which
the actual sermon was built than a full verbatim copy
of the sermon as delivered. It is not usual to print
sermons without careful revision, especially from a
doctrinal point of view. To print sermons, for in-
stance, found after the writer’s death without a clear
intimation on his part that they were intended to be
printed is to incur the risk of misrepresenting his
views and intentions to the world. One’s private
copies of his sermons delivered, or to be <elivered, are
often incomplete and inaccurate, and generally not in-
tended for the eyes of others. There is a true story
-of an Anglican clergyman named Baker, whose sermons
found after his death contained directions on the
mergin as to the mode of delivery suited to particular
passages. In the case of doctrinal passages, which re-
-quired precision and thought rather than emotion, there
was written on the margin, ‘‘ Steady, Baker!”’ but in
the case of emotional passages requiring heat and
vehemence in delivery the marginal note ran, ‘ Go it,
Baker !’ >

When, therefore, the circumstances connected with
“the sermons we are reviewing are considered, it is im-

. possible not to approve of the taste and judgment shown
in the selection made by the editors. The sermons, on
_ the whole, are interesting reading. The present writer
_ teok a particular interest in the sermon on ‘‘ The Life
of St. Patrick >’ in the first volume, and also in-]a. se]:-

Though these sermons, on the w]}ole,’ may not serve
as good models for present-day discourses, they will
serve a useful purpose in opening up to the student and
intending preacher some of the leading subjects of
which he will have to treat. They will also form useful
reading for the adult laity who can read Irish, especially
in the Irish districts. There is a great and distressing
dearth of printed religious literature in Irish, and until
that dearth is removed the problem of preaching in
Trish will be a difficult one. Meantime one welcomes
every elfort made to remove it, and not the least wel-
come certainly is the series of sermons that recall the
sincere piety and unfeigned zeal of our ancestors in an
age when to be a’ Catholic was to be a serf, and when
the preacher was an outlaw, and with a price on his
head.

It may, I think, be assumed that the best sermons,
from an oratorical point of view, delivered during the
period covered by the series in these volumes were
never committed to writing, or, at most, were confined
to scrappy notes which are now lost. If it be true, as
has been stated on high authority, that not even a
single Catholic parish register or record between the
years 1700 and 1740 can now be found, it is no wonder
{hat written sermons for the period should be practi-
cally non-existent. 5

I hope the young editors of these volumes will not
relax their efforts, but will make a further effort to
supply the extreme want there is of popular religious
literature in Irish.
